stocks.note: Numbers as of market close April 20th.
stocks.news:
All three indexes slipped Monday as a U.S. destroyer fired on an Iranian cargo ship in the Gulf of Oman. Oil took off, equities leaked, and the market mood flipped from cautious to jumpy in a single session.

Bezos backed another $650M round for Slate Auto, the stripped-down EV pickup with hand-crank windows and no touchscreen. 160,000 deposits say buyers want cheap, not loaded. Separately, Uber (UBER +0.48%) committed $10B to owning robotaxis after a decade of swearing it never would. Cars without drivers still beat cars with them.

economy.note: US 10-Year and Gold as of 4:00 PM ET April 20th; WTI Crude reflects today's intraday move via investing.com.
economy.commentary:
Oil popped nearly 8% intraday after the Strait of Hormuz reopened as a flashpoint. About 20% of the world's oil moves through that corridor. When it slows, gas prices rise, inflation sticks, and the Fed has a harder time cutting rates. The chain matters for housing more than the equity reaction.

The $166B tariff refund portal finally opened two months after the Supreme Court stopped President Trump's sweeping tariffs. Importers can file, but White House adviser Kevin Hassett already hinted the checks may come back smaller than expected. Kind of like waiting on that Venmo from the friend who always "forgets."

China just restarted a $3.7B coal plant it shelved a decade ago. Gas got pricey when the Strait closed, so Beijing is reaching for the fuel it has tons of. A 21-month streak of flat-to-falling emissions is about to break.

crypto.note: Numbers as of 4:00 PM ET on April 20th.
crypto.commentary:
Crypto ran green while equities slid, the classic "flight from geopolitics" trade. Central bankers just flagged U.S. stablecoins as a $315B problem. About 98% are pegged to the dollar, which is great for U.S. reach but bad news for places like Egypt and Pakistan where people now use them as savings accounts. Brazil already capped stablecoin transfers at $100K and other countries are watching.

Consensus, the biggest crypto conference of the year, kicks off May 5 in Miami. Both SEC and CFTC chiefs will be on stage. Why it matters: when regulators talk, crypto moves. Token prices historically drop about 12% the week after SEC enforcement news.

real_estate.homebuilder: Rates ticked up again with MBS prices moderately weaker. Nothing dramatic on the tape, but the oil spike is the real story. If the Strait of Hormuz stays tight, inflation stays sticky, and the Fed's path to rate cuts stretches out.
real_estate.commentary:
The oil-and-rates headline is the conversation to have with sellers waiting on "rates to drop." Sub-6% is not guaranteed. Price now for today's buyer pool, not a 5.99% fantasy. Homes priced at or just under the last comp are still moving in about 2 weeks. Overpriced ones are sitting 60+.

📍 Alameda & Contra Costa Snapshot (04/21/26)

📍 Fremont: 47 new. Still the anchor of Alameda County inventory.

📍 Union City: 7 new. Steady flow. One of the tightest pools on the board.

📍 Newark: 7 new. Mid-tier activity holding.

📍 Hayward: 19 new. Biggest fresh supply jump among the I-880 cities.

📍 Milpitas: 4 new. Low fresh supply. Pending count of 31 townhomes signals real absorption.

📍 Pleasanton: 18 new. Tri-Valley demand steady.

📍 Dublin: 25 new. Fresh supply picking up.

📍 San Ramon: 23 new. Balanced.

📍 Livermore: 22 new. East Tri-Valley still active.

📍 Castro Valley: 12 new. Quiet but moving.

📍 Danville: 26 new. Strongest Contra Costa entry.

📍 San Leandro: 12 new. Consistent.

📍 San Lorenzo: 4 new. Minimal new stock.

📍 Oakland: 58 new. Volume leader by far.

Rate Expectations: Zillow is flagging a 30-year near 6.02% and about to slip under 6% for the first time since February. Classic market psychology. 5.99% feels like a deal. 6.02% doesn't. That tiny shift could thaw a housing market frozen for two years. The catch? It only holds if the Iran story does.

Apartment demand went negative in Q4, with renters handing back 40,000 more units than they signed. The hinge group? 20-to-24-year-olds. Slow job growth in that cohort and a record share still living with parents are keeping first-time formation on the sidelines. Another reason starter-home inventory matters.
